title: Complexity of tensor network simulation for noisy quantum circuits
authors: Yuguo Shao, Zishuo Zhao, Song Cheng, Zhengwei Liu
abstract: We aim to rigorously address how local noise affects classical simulability of quantum dynamics benchmarked by tensor-network methods. Using operator entanglement entropy (OEE), we prove the following: (1) For single-qubit depolarizing noise on arbitrary circuits, tensor networks with $\mathrm{poly}(n)$ bond dimension suffice for fixed absolute Hilbert-Schmidt error after $\order{1}$ depth, while relative error demands $\order{\log n}$ depth; and this bound is optimal. (2) For single-qubit depolarizing noise on 1D local circuits, the existence of whole-trajectory error-bounded matrix product operator (MPO) of $\mathrm{poly}(n)$ bond dimension at all depths. (3) For general single-qubit noise in 1D brickwall circuits, random two-design gates with contraction coefficient $c<1/3$ yield an $\order{1}$ OEE plateau with probability $1-Te^{-Ω(n)}$, while arbitrary gates with $c<1/48$ give $\order{\log n}$ OEE in the worst case. (4) In higher dimensions, these bounds yield uniform-in-depth $\mathrm{poly}(n)$ average boundary-bond dimensions for projected entangled pair operators~(PEPO) across every cut -- under depolarizing noise at either absolute or relative accuracy, and under general noise with strong contraction at absolute accuracy. Our results establish a rigorous connection between certain noise models, circuit types, and their classical simulability.
summary: This paper provides a rigorous theoretical framework to determine the classical simulability of noisy quantum circuits by analyzing the Operator Entanglement Entropy (OEE). It proves that for specific noise types, the required bond dimension for tensor network simulation remains polynomially bounded in system size, establishing a deep link between noise structure and computational complexity.
main_problem: The paper rigorously analyzes how local noise affects the classical simulability of quantum dynamics, benchmarking this using tensor-network methods.
main_result: It establishes that for certain noise models (like depolarizing noise), the required bond dimension for simulation remains polynomial in system size, even at deep circuit depths, connecting noise characteristics to classical tractability.
method: The analysis heavily relies on Operator Entanglement Entropy (OEE) as a diagnostic tool, deriving rigorous bounds on the required bond dimension ($\chi$) based on absolute ($\epsilon$) or relative ($\delta$) error tolerances.
model_or_system: The system under study is noisy quantum circuits, modeled by sequential application of local gates and single-qubit noise channels (e.g., depolarizing or amplitude damping noise). The simulation framework uses Matrix Product Operators (MPO) and Projected Entangled Pair Operators (PEPO).
key_observables: Operator Entanglement Entropy (SOE and eSOE), required bond dimension ($\chi$), and the contraction coefficient ($c$ or $\eta$).
important_parameters: Circuit depth ($L$), system size ($n$), error tolerances ($\epsilon, \delta$), and noise parameters (e.g., damping parameter $\gamma$).
main_assumptions: The analysis often assumes specific noise models (e.g., depolarizing noise) or utilizes averaging over random gates (2-design) to achieve the best bounds.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the scaling of OEE with depth for depolarizing noise, showing plateaus or logarithmic growth depending on the error metric and noise type.
paper_structure: The paper builds from defining the OEE measures (SOE vs eSOE) and their relationship, to applying these measures to analyze the effect of noise on the required bond dimension for simulating 1D and higher-dimensional circuits.
why_interesting: This work provides concrete, quantitative thresholds (e.g., $c < 1/3$ or $c < 1/48$) that dictate when noisy quantum evolution remains classically simulable via tensor networks, which is crucial for understanding the limits of quantum computation.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Depolarizing noise guarantees polynomial bond di- mension of TN simulation at all circuit depths. For arbitrary local circuits, the bond dimension is controlled by locality be- fore the respective crossover and by the noise-induced OEE thresholds after it: Labs = O(1) for absolute Hilbert-Schmidt error and Lrel = Θ(log n) for relative error, both geometry independent.
Figure 2. The maximum operator entanglement entropy Smax OE (t) and its normalized version eSmax OE (t) with respect to the purity t when DA = DB = 4.
Figure 3. Schematic of a 1D circuit with nearest-neighbor two-qubit gates and noise applied after each layer of unitary gates. The noise is drawn in red color.
